facts.text: East Islip’s name traces back to 1890 when it was christened after William Nicoll’s estate, an English aristocrat who bought 51,000 acres in 1683.
The East Islip Historical Society, founded in 1967, preserves the town’s story through a vast archive of photographs, maps, and documents that span from farming days to modern suburbia.
Brookwood Hall, once a private estate of the Thorne family, now serves as the Town of Islip’s municipal building and is a centerpiece of local heritage.
The society rescued the Long Island Railroad lean‑to at Great River station and helped rebuild Brookwood Hall’s Gatehouse after a 1995 fire.
East Islip’s historic Brookwood Hall Gatehouse was redesigned by an architect chosen for his historically conscious approach, thanks to the society’s recommendation.
The town’s museum, housed by the East Islip Historical Society, showcases artifacts that chronicle East Islip’s evolution from a farming community to a suburban enclave.
